title: Share of Caribbean reefs with Acropora corals present or dominant
subtitle: The share of coral reefs in the Caribbean where major reef-building group of corals, _Acropora_, was dominant or present. The declines show the loss of coral cover as a result of pressures including water pollution, disease outbreaks, and [coral bleaching](#dod:coral_bleaching).
caption:
Share of Caribbean reefs with Acropora corals present or dominant
The share of coral reefs in the Caribbean where major reef-building group of corals, Acropora, was dominant or present. The declines show the loss of coral cover as a result of pressures including water pollution, disease outbreaks, and coral bleaching.

Across the Caribbean between 1950 and 2007, the prevalence of Acropora corals on reefs shows a sustained downward trajectory, measured as the percentage of reefs where the genus was either present or dominant. The chart plots two distinct metrics over this period: the share of reefs where Acropora was present at all, represented by the upper line, and the share where it was the dominant species, shown by the lower line. In 1950, the presence of these corals was recorded at approximately 90%, while dominance stood near 80%. Over the subsequent decades, both metrics experienced significant reductions. The line tracking presence drops to roughly 65% by the mid-1960s, recovers slightly to near 75% around 1975, and then falls steeply to approximately 20% by 2007. The dominance metric follows a similar but more severe path, falling from its 1950 high to roughly 45% by 1965, rising briefly to 60% in the mid-1970s, and then declining to below 10% by the end of the timeline. The data excludes international aviation and figures represent the loss of coral cover associated with water pollution, disease outbreaks, and coral bleaching. By the final recorded year, the gap between the two lines narrows significantly, with presence hovering just under 20% and dominance falling to roughly 5%. The visual pattern displays a consistent long-term reduction in both the presence and dominance of Acropora corals across the region.
